title: How To Fix An Asymmetrical Smile With Botox.
filename: How To Fix An Asymmetrical Smile With Botox..en.vtt
excerpt: How do you fix a crooked smile so crooked Smiles are actually quite common and sometimes it's just natural and sometimes it's because you've just caused it with a botel toxin injection and both of them can be managed in a similar way we need to understand first and foremost the a…

segments.text: how do you fix a crooked smile so crooked Smiles are actually quite common and sometimes it's just natural and sometimes it's because you've just caused it with a botel toxin injection and both of them can be managed in a similar way we need to understand first and foremost the anatomy that's causing the problem so crooked Smiles overall very complicated because what you're really talking about is asymmetry of the entire lower face you may have a patient with a crooked smile because their cheekbone is not the same on one side or the other and the muscle attachments are there for pulling in a different way they may have a much Fuller fat pad on one side which means the muscles are resisted by that action they may also have dental work that's affecting their smile so don't jump into treating the muscles of the lower face just because someone has a symmetry without thinking about the whole complicated 3D structure of the lower face that's why most of this video will be about the muscles that we can treat but it has to start with a proper assessment considering all the other structures let's have a look at the anatomy that's involved in smiling now if you've heard that joke or that saying that it takes more muscles

that saying that it takes more muscles

to frown than to smile you may change your mind after this video let's have a look at what happens when you smile all of these muscles here in the lower face are activated so it starts with the levor Lei elesi muscle but also Leva Lei superioris zygomaticus minor zygomaticus major rorius are all contributing to the up and lateral pull of the top half of the mouth and then the muscles underneath the lip are also involved we have action from depressor angularis orus in some patients and certainly a lot of depressed Ali in most people because really what's happening is the whole oral aperture is being pulled open so the lower muscles pull down and out and the upper muscles pull up and out and so all of the muscles of the lower face are active to some degree when you're smiling and all of the muscles can therefore affect asymmetry what we're most often presented with though is lower lip asymmetry it tends to be worse and for some reason more distracting and it's also the one that we so often cause with injections why does the depressed labii muscle get

orus in some patients and certainly a

because really what's happening is the
whole oral aperture is being pulled open
so the lower muscles pull down and out

and so all of the muscles of the lower
face are active to some degree when
you're smiling and all of the muscles
can therefore affect asymmetry what
we're most often presented with though
is lower lip asymmetry it tends to be
worse and for some reason more
distracting and it's also the one that
we so often cause with injections why
does the depressed labii muscle get

affected so often by boty toxin treatments well if we have a look at the anatomy it is intwined with two of the muscles that we treat on purpose the first medially is the mentalis which you can see on this diagram have a look at how the mentalis passes underneath the depressi muscle you can see when you look at this diagram how if you are superficial to the muscle or if you're too Superior you will overlap with the depressi muscle which means you will no longer be able to pull that lower lip outwards away from the teeth which means you have a symmetry on that side next you can have a look at depressor angular asaurus so this muscle is pulling the corner of the mouth down and we are often trying to relax just a portion of it so that we get a little bit more upward pull through the muscles that normally pull the cheek upwards so if you're injecting depressor angular asaurus you are very close to the muscle underneath which is depress Albia you can see in these two diagrams what a strong overlap there is you can also see that the least amount of overlap happens on the lateral border and intermediately between the oral Comm measure and the angle of the jaw this is why my

preferred place to inject is halfway between the modiolus and the angle of the jaw and on the lateral border because it's the furthest place from the depress Lai many other injectors do different techniques but this is my preferred way to do it when your patient presents with an asymmetrical smile the first thing to do is to compare it with all the muscles that you've now learned on the face and figure out which muscles are out of balance you can for example cause an asymmetrical smile that's caused by treatment of the depressor angularis orus on one side more than the other and they just smile differently it's not quite the same thing as the more common complication or aesthetic side effect which is treatment of the depress Ali by accident so the depressor labii pulls the more medial part of the lip Down and Out whereas depressor angular asaurus is more about lateral the oral commisure being pulled down so when you see your patient who's saying they have asymmetry try and figure out which one of those muscles has been affected of course there are other reasons for asymmetry as well so for example if you've done botam toxin in the upper face you may affect if you're way too deep you can affect the zy I is Major and that means they cannot smile on one side pulling up which is

depressor Lai is most medial and underneath it my Approach is to treat halfway between the modiolus and the point of insertion where it comes from the mandible and to be on the lateral border of that structure and I do this by finding it on my patient when I ask them to contract and then I feel with my finger for the lateral border and then I mark on that structure so you can literally feel a line that comes straight down here because really what you've got here is an empty space underneath the rorius where you're trying to inject I also use quite small doses for the first time I've treated a patient so 2 units maximum and that's usually enough to keep me safe and symmetrical I also am a believer that it's better to try and get that sense that you're going into the muscle rather than into the fat because I think a lot of asymmetry happens when toxin is just too superficial if you have a layer of fat which there often is here in patients it's easy to lose toxin so I'm trying to aim for the muscle not for some near it uh in the fat if you'd like to get a summary of this whole video just click the second link in the description of this video
